title: Man, 28, charged with murder of Ann Widdecombe
description: A 28-year-old man has been charged with the murder of Ann Widdecombe, police and prosecutors have said.Joshua Kerry, from Rotherham in South Yorkshire
body: A 28-year-old man has been charged with the murder of Ann Widdecombe, police and prosecutors have said.Joshua Kerry, from Rotherham in South Yorkshire, is set to appear at Westminster Magistrates' Court on Tuesday.Counter Terrorism Policing said "determining the motivation, including any possible political motivation" remains "an active avenue" of investigation.Kerry was initially arrested on suspicion of murder on 11 July, before being additionally arrested on 13 July on suspicion of the commission, preparation or instigation of acts of terrorism.Widdecombe, 78, a former Conservative minister and Reform UK spokeswoman, was found dead on Thursday 9 July at her home in Haytor, Devon, having sustained serious injuries.In a statement read outside New Scotland Yard, Laurence Taylor, head of UK Counter Terrorism Policing said: "I want to make it very clear that our investigation into [Kerry], determining his motivations and any wider activity he may have been involved in is continuing.""As with all murder investigations, understanding the 'why' is a key part of our enquiries," he said, adding that it was "a particularly intensive and complex investigation".Taylor said it remained the case that investigators were not looking for anyone else in connection with the incident.He added that counter terrorism teams have gathered "a huge amount of information" over the last week, leading to more than 800 lines of enquiry.The investigation, which remains ongoing, has been led by Counter Terrorism Policing South East, supported by Devon and Cornwall Police.Frank Ferguson, of the Crown Prosecution Service (CPS)'s special crime and counter terrorism division, said: "Our prosecutors have worked closely with the police to establish that there is sufficient evidence to bring this case to court, and we will continue working with them as their investigation progresses.""We remind all concerned that proceedings against the defendant are active and he has the right to a fair trial," Ferguson said."It is vital that there should be no reporting, commentary or sharing of information which could in anyway prejudice these proceedings."

schema.headline: Man, 28, charged with murder of Ann Widdecombe

title: Trump slaps 50% tariffs on Canada and Carney vows to 'intensify' trade talks
description: US President Donald Trump has imposed a 50% tariff on a wide range of goods imported from Canada, in retaliation for what he called "unequal treatment
body: US President Donald Trump has imposed a 50% tariff on a wide range of goods imported from Canada, in retaliation for what he called "unequal treatment" of US cars, dairy and alcohol. Everyday consumer items like wine and hockey sticks and industrial goods such as cement are among the goods targeted. However, several key exports will be spared, such as energy, potash, critical minerals and fish. Prime Minister Mark Carney responded by saying Canada stood ready to "intensify" trade talks with US in the coming weeks.The White House said the duties would take effect in 30 days. It marks a major escalation in trade tensions between the North American neighbours. The new duties apply to all covered goods regardless of whether the product was included under the existing free trade agreement between Canada, the US and Mexico, known as the USMCA."This is the latest in a series of unilateral US trade actions that began with the US imposing a series of tariffs in direct violation of the Canada-United States-Mexico Agreement," Carney said in a statement on X.He also cited "threats to Canadian sovereignty", a possible reference to Trump's calls to make America's northern neighbour the 51st US state.Monday's import taxes build on trade barriers already in place between the two nations. The US has been maintaining active tariffs ranging from 15% to 50% on Canadian steel, aluminium and copper. Washington also charges a 35% tariff on Canadian softwood lumber, alongside a 25% tax on non-US parts in cars. Canada has its own 25% counter-tariff on selected imports of American steel, aluminium and vehicles. Monday's announcement comes in the wake of President Trump's threat to impose tariffs over Canadian wildfire smoke drifting into US cities.But there is no mention of wildfires in the executive orders that Trump signed on Monday.Instead, the three proclamations list US trade irritants that were previously known to Canada related to cars, dairy and alcohol - signalling a breakdown of trade negotiations between the two countries.On cars, Trump is accusing Canada of charging a tax on US motor vehicles and parts that are not covered under USMCA.He argues it is "unreasonable" and that Canada has discriminated against the US by not charging other countries a similar tax.Automotive manufacturing in North America is highly integrated between Canada, the US and Mexico.But Trump's Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick has said in the past that he believes Canada should "come second" to the US. Trump has also named cars in the past as one issue where the two countries have competing interests.Dairy, meanwhile, has long been a problem for the US, specifically Canada's supply management system, which sets limits on foreign imports. Those that exceed the limit are charged a tariff upwards of 300%.And lastly, the enduring boycott of US alcoholic drinks by most Canadian provinces has become a major sore point for the Americans since it was imposed last year. Canadian premiers have said repeatedly that the boycott will be lifted if the US removes its tariffs on key Canadian sectors, including metals and automobiles.Canadian trade negotiators have been trying to secure a deal that would at least reduce some of the current US tariffs.The BBC has contacted the White House and the Canadian government for comment.Earlier this year, the US chose not to renew the USMCA in its current form.Canada and Mexico sought a renewal of the trade agreement, but the US wants to make changes to the deal, which was negotiated during Trump's first term in office.As it stands, the treaty will continue to govern North American trade over the next decade on a rolling basis, requiring annual reviews.In February, the US Supreme Court struck down sweeping international tariffs imposed by Trump through the International Emergency Economic Powers Act of 1977.The justices ruled that the president had exceeded his authority when he announced the duties under a law reserved for national emergencies. The White House vowed at the time that it would invoke other mechanisms to impose import taxes. The duties announced on Monday were introduced under Section 338 of the 1930 Tariff Act, which covers trade discrimination rather than national emergencies. Candance Laing, head of the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, called the latest tariffs a "regrettable decision", and urged officials to make "meaningful progress" in talks before the new duties take effect in 30 days' time.Chris Swonger, head of the Distilled Spirits Council of the United States, also called for both sides to find a solution, warning the decision "raises the risk of further retaliation".

schema.headline: Trump slaps 50% tariffs on Canada and Carney vows to 'intensify' trade talks

title: UK's furry bumblebees 'keeling over' in heatwaves
description: The impact of heatwaves is two-fold for bumblebees.UK species are adapted to our historically cooler climate - with fat, furry bodies that help them r
body: The impact of heatwaves is two-fold for bumblebees.UK species are adapted to our historically cooler climate - with fat, furry bodies that help them retain heat and powerful flight muscles that warm them as they fly.But in a heatwave their muscles generate more heat, while their thick coats and round bodies make it harder to cool down.Dr Richard Comont, the Trust's science manager, says that once air temperatures reach around 28C to 32C, UK bumblebees can start to struggle to fly without overheating. If they cannot fly, they cannot forage."They actually cook themselves from the inside out," says Comont.  "Once their internal body temperature is much above 40C, they literally start dying, they start keeling over."The BeeWalk data shows in 2022, when the UK had three official summer heatwaves and temperatures reached 40.3C, population numbers dropped significantly. Red-tailed, buff-tailed and common carder bumblebees - three of the country's most common species - peaked well below normal levels - down 20%, 25% and 35% respectively - before dropping further into autumn as colonies struggled.The effects appeared to last beyond that summer. BeeWalk data showed overall queen numbers were below average in March, April and May 2023.Scientists stress that warm, sunny weather is not automatically bad for bumblebees. Previous warm years have been good for some species, particularly those with more southerly ranges.The problem is extreme heat and drought. As well as their overheating bodies, the other issue for bumblebees is dwindling supplies of food.Colonies depend on workers bringing back a steady supply of nectar and pollen through the summer. If flowering plants begin to dry up, they produce less.Dr Paul Williams, a bumblebee expert at the Natural History Museum, says foraging is an energy calculation for bumblebees. The nectar return has to make the expenditure of energy worthwhile. As he puts it, they need to "make a profit".They cannot afford to waste energy searching dried-up flowers for nectar and pollen that is no longer there.If they cannot find enough food, larvae develop more slowly, colonies grow less strongly and fewer new queens may be produced for the following year. Rising global temperatures, as a result of climate change, has put the UK on course for more frequent and intense heatwaves.Conservationists say the best way to help is by creating more flower-rich habitats, so bumblebees can find food even as the climate becomes more extreme.Dr Sarah Beynon is an insect expert whose west Wales farm is managed for wildlife as well as food. She has planted meadows, willows, hedgerows, tussocky grasses and late-flowering crops to give insects food through the summer."When we came, this farm was a wildlife desert," she says. "In 10 years, it's become a nature reserve."Her message is that people do not need acres of land to help. A pot of native wildflowers on a doorstep or windowsill can act as a small refuelling stop for insects moving through a human-dominated landscape."Your flowerpot is a service station," she says. "It's somewhere to rest, refuel and carry on."  Dr Richard Comont adds that putting in a pond - however small - and shade can also help.Flowers growing near water tend to produce nectar with more moisture in it, he says. Bumblebees cannot collect water in the way honeybees do, they get most of the liquid they need from nectar, so wetter nectar can help make colonies more resilient in hot weather.Whilst planting dense shrubs gives bumblebees places to escape the heat of the sun, and can allow them to move between flowers through cooler patches of air.

schema.headline: UK's furry bumblebees 'keeling over' in heatwaves

title: Nations Championship: England's Ben Earl calls for brutal change after "brutal" schedule
description: Ben Earl has called for a change to the Nations Championship format after England's "brutal" schedule in the summer leg of the inaugural competition.E
body: Ben Earl has called for a change to the Nations Championship format after England's "brutal" schedule in the summer leg of the inaugural competition.England had to play three Tests in three different continents on successive weekends and accumulated 25,000 miles of travel.Victory over Argentina on Saturday keeps Steve Borthwick's team in the hunt for a place in the final on 29 November at Twickenham's Allianz Stadium.However, four long haul and four short haul flights in the past four weeks have contributed to players losing sleep and a reduced training schedule."It's been brutal, there's no two ways about it," England number 8 Earl said."We've been speaking about it – why would we not all be playing in one country? So you just don't travel, you go to one nation and have that festival feel."I'm not paid to make those decisions but it has been tough. It's been tough coming at the end of what's been quite a long season anyway for most of us."It's been back and forth, coming back to England, seeing your families and then going away again."It has seemed a bit weird but it's just something we're going to have to get used to because I think it's just the beginning."The competition resumes later in the year for the autumn leg with England next in action against Australia on 8 November.

schema.headline: Nations Championship: England's Ben Earl calls for brutal change after "brutal" schedule

title: Cull of Starmer allies as new Chancellor faces crunching spending choices
description: A government ends and a new government begins, determined to make a splash with its early policy announcements.We can expect to see and hear plenty fr
body: A government ends and a new government begins, determined to make a splash with its early policy announcements.We can expect to see and hear plenty from the new Prime Minister Andy Burnham and his senior ministers in the coming days, making arguments, travelling the country and trying to make an impact.Burnham's new cabinet will meet for the first time at lunchtime.Its appointment became something of a nocturnal affair.At gone 22:00 BST on Monday night, there was still a minibus full of ministerial wannabes inside Downing Street waiting for their new positions to be confirmed.One of the later posts to be filled was that of defence secretary, a role taken by Wes Streeting, whose resignation as health secretary two months ago contributed to the draining of Sir Keir Starmer's authority as prime minister.The story of the reshuffle can best be summarised as neither of the two ministers most widely talked up as chancellor - Shabana Mahmood and Ed Miliband - getting that job, it instead going to John Healey. And there being a pretty significant cull of the ministers closest to and most loyal to the now former prime minister: think David Lammy, Darren Jones, Peter Kyle, Lord Hermer, Steve Reed and Rachel Reeves – albeit Reeves, I am told, was offered a cabinet post but turned it down.The appointment of Healey as chancellor is fascinating. Healey was a minister in the Treasury for five years between 2002 and 2007, firstly as economic secretary and then as financial secretary.Senior figures in government say Burnham and Healey share the same economic outlook and ambitions – built on a desire for reindustrialisation and devolution.It is also true that just a matter of weeks ago the new chancellor was being remarkably barbed about the government department he now leads.He talked of a "Treasury orthodoxy that's a dead hand on dynamic government" and that it saw "defence as a drain rather than a driver of growth."He added, with sting as a resigning defence secretary, that "our adversaries do not follow timetables set by the Treasury."As one figure in Whitehall, steeped in defence, texted to remind me, Healey wrote in his resignation letter that "I am certain that a headmark date for 3% of GDP on defence in 2030 is what Britain must set."Now it is his job, as chancellor, to make those numbers add up.And not just those numbers.

schema.headline: Cull of Starmer allies as new Chancellor faces crunching spending choices

title: £100,000 of TikTok sales in a day: The business of live shopping
description: Daisy's business goes live from a studio for a minimum of six hours a day with different presenters interacting with shoppers, from showing off produc
body: Daisy's business goes live from a studio for a minimum of six hours a day with different presenters interacting with shoppers, from showing off products to answering their questions."It's like having 500 people in your shop," she says.There are a host of platforms and marketplaces now offering livestreams connecting sellers and customers, from Instagram Live, YouTube Shopping, to eBay and Amazon Live. They're all different.Live shopping, it seems, is becoming part of everyday consumer behaviour for many.According to new research from retail agency Savvy Marketing, 30% of shoppers surveyed said they'd bought something from a live shopping event."The big retailers have got to grab hold of this," says Catherine Shuttleworth, CEO of Savvy. "It's grown from nothing to a huge thing really quickly. If you haven't got a strategy for live selling, you're going to miss out."TikTok Shop says its UK sales grew by more than 30% year-on-year in June, with live shopping the fastest-growing format.It says it now has more than 6,000 livestreams taking place every day.At an event in the centuries old Covent Garden market, it hosted 20 small businesses with stalls who were also selling live to customers all over the UK.With nothing more than a smartphone, a lighting kit and a fridge full of fresh meat, halal butcher Manny Malik from Wolverhampton had around two hundred people tuning into his session."Last week we did a TikTok live for three hours and we sold £8,000 worth of meat," he says.It would normally take him three to four days to sell this amount in his family shop, Malik Butchers.They're now opening a second store in Stoke. TikTok, he says, is helping to drive his expansion.But there are fees.TikTok Shop UK keeps up to 9p from every £1 sold on the platform, as its standard commission. As a comparison, eBay's marketplace business sellers typically pay between 6.9p and 14.9p depending on the category. Businesses selling on the dedicated live selling format, eBay Live, pay a combined fee of up to 8.42p including payment processing."It's a lot," says Manny."For us in the meat game, there aren't huge margins. However, it's opened up a new platform. We didn't have these sales prior to TikTok. We're not losing money, we've got enough profitability in there," he adds.Buket Sevik, 34, founded her jewellery brand Adorina in February after quitting her 9-5 job as a business manager. Her new business went from 50 orders a month to 500 orders a week since she started live selling on TikTok UK shop in May. She's gone from working from her dining table to live streaming from a small unit, where orders are now being packed."It's crazy," she laughs.Demand is so high, she's trying to hire someone to help.But for Buket, the best thing about livestreaming is the flexibility. "I can go live whenever I want... which means it works absolutely perfectly for me as a mum. I can put my daughter to sleep and I can just hit go live and do some sales."Savvy's latest survey of shoppers also found that 66% of Gen Y and Gen Z shoppers said seeing products sell out or go viral made them more likely to buy.That's something that worries the free debt advice service, Money Wellness, which warns live shopping events are "a recipe for overspending", says external relations manager Rebecca Lamb."Every time you snap up a bargain before it sells out, your brain gets a dopamine hit - the same rush you get from gambling," she says."And because a lot offer one tap payment, there's no card, no basket and no total. Often you've spent money before your brain has caught up," she says.

schema.headline: £100,000 of TikTok sales in a day: The business of live shopping

title: Your tips on getting a fussy child to eat, and six recipes they'll love
description: Image source, Getty ImagesByPhilippa RoxbyHealth reporterIt's the start of the summer holidays and you've already had five battles with your child to
body: Image source, Getty ImagesByPhilippa RoxbyHealth reporterIt's the start of the summer holidays and you've already had five battles with your child to get them to eat a bit of broccoli. For many parents, these few weeks can be a stressful time of the year at the dinner table. So how should we deal with our picky eaters? The BBC asked you for your tips on making mealtimes a more enjoyable experience.Encourage eating veg by changing how you serve themMum-of-four Natalia, from south-west London, makes veggie nuggets for her children."They like green beans but not by themselves, so we just fry them in a little bit of butter and dip in breadcrumbs. The same with cauliflower and lots of other vegetables," she says.Teacher Kieron Fisher, who lives in Newbury, Berkshire, includes vegetables in meals his six-year-old twins Kit and Ollie already enjoy, such as curries and spaghetti Bolognese."It helps increase their vegetable intake without causing a battle."Image source, Kieron FisherImage caption, Kieron's twins Kit and Ollie can be "quite fussy at times" but picnicking in the park is always a hitEmma Robins, from Reading, says broccoli hidden in pesto pasta is a favourite for her three-year-old son Alfred, as well as veg pulsed into pasta sauces. She also recommends making your own burgers with vegetables.A lack of routine in the holidays can be particularly challenging for some children, so Kieron suggests using visual boards showing the healthy snack they are about to have and another showing the activity they are going to do next.Children with special educational needs can sometimes experience sensory difficulties when it comes to food - and are not classified as fussy eaters. Some of these suggestions may not be suitable for them.Don't force them to finish everything on their plate"If they're hungry, they'll eat. I don't force them to because I think that can instil pressure," says Doone, from London. She says her daughter Cora, two, used to be the "best eater" but seeing others at nursery turn their noses up at certain foods made her do the same."I'm trying not to make a big deal of it. Even though they don't eat it, I always put the broccoli on the plate so that they're seeing it."Emma suggests giving lots of options, so that kids think they are getting to choose. In Alfred's lunchbox, she always adds two or three healthy options.And she says to praise them when they take an interest. "If they say one day they want carrots, run and get the carrots and give big praise when they eat them," Emma says.Keep offering food until they try it  It may take up to 15 attempts before kids learn to like new foods, research suggests."Keep going," says one mum outside Barnes Primary School, in south-west London. "Keep offering it and they will pick it up and try it. We try to make everyone try everything in our house, even the tiniest bit."Sometimes it can be the colour or texture of a certain food that puts them off, another parent says, until they actually try it.Children refusing to eat new foods or rejecting ones they've always liked is not unusual. More than half will show fussy eating habits at some point, the NHS says, external.Vegetables, in particular, can taste quite bitter to young palates. Most habits will improve over time.But if your kids start to avoid certain foods and limit how much they eat in an unhealthy way, it's worth seeking medical advice, external.Make eating a family affair - and get the portion size right"I always eat with my girls and I eat what they're eating," says Doone, who has two "quite fussy eaters".The British Nutrition Foundation agrees that food should be a family affair - allowing children to learn from what their parents do. When a bit older, they can help with preparation too.Emma adds to the fun by taking her kids fruit picking and also by letting them choose at the supermarket, then having tasty picnics in the garden.Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, The British Nutrition Foundation says food should be a family affair - allowing children to learn from what their parents doBut first thing's first, says Kieron: think about the portion size."I make sure their plates aren't too full or overcrowded, as too much food at once can feel overwhelming," he says.Serve snacks alongside healthier options Doone says: "I am making a conscious effort not to give many snacks because then they're more hungry at dinner time."Natalia leaves out healthy finger food, like chopped peppers and apples, for her children to pick at.Image caption, Finger food is easy to pick at Don't completely ban them, says Kieron - just serve snacks alongside healthier options, like berries or other fruit, replicate snack times at school and don't stress about the odd treat."Six weeks [of summer holidays] is a long time, so it's about focusing on the overall balance of their diet rather than aiming for perfection every day," he says.

schema.headline: Your tips on getting a fussy child to eat, and six recipes they'll love

title: Will the Tate brothers be extradited from the US to the UK?
description: The CPS said on Sunday that Andrew was being charged with seven further counts of rape, three counts of arranging or facilitating trafficking for sexu
body: The CPS said on Sunday that Andrew was being charged with seven further counts of rape, three counts of arranging or facilitating trafficking for sexual exploitation, three counts of assault occasioning actual bodily harm and 19 additional charges for offences relating to indecent images of a child and extreme pornography.Tristan's new charges include two counts of rape, one count of sexual assault and three counts of arranging or facilitating trafficking for sexual exploitation.The alleged offending is said to have taken place between July 2010 and August 2017.Andrew now faces a total of 42 charges in the UK and Tristan faces 17 charges, after CPS had pursued other charges last year."These charging decisions followed receipt of a further file of evidence from Bedfordshire Police and bring the total number of alleged victims in this case to seven," Malcolm McHaffie, Head of the Special Crime Division at the CPS, said.The brothers have been based in Romania, where they are also under criminal investigation. In 2024, Bedfordshire Police secured European arrest warrants for the pair to have them returned from Romania when proceedings against them there conclude. They deny all allegations against them. The brothers have been able to travel to the US since travel restrictions against them in Romania were lifted in early 2025.A US Department of Justice spokesperson said the arrests were "pursuant to extradition proceedings".The UK and the US are parties to a treaty that allows the extradition of people in relation to alleged offences punishable under laws in both countries by at least one year in prison.For a UK case, the CPS prepares a request which is certified by a judge. The final request is sent to be considered by the Home Office, which then forwards the extradition request to the relevant state through usual diplomatic channels.The decision to accept the extradition request is taken by the US State Department. The request will then be considered by the US courts, with a final decision made by US Secretary of State Marco Rubio.Appearing in a federal courtroom in Miami on Monday, the pair wore light brown prison uniforms and were handcuffed and shackled at the waist and ankles.They spoke only when asked if they understood their rights to which they replied together "yes your honour".The brothers' attorney, Joe McBride, told reporters after the brief hearing that his clients are innocent."They've never done nothing wrong. They shouldn't be extradited for crimes they didn't commit," McBride said."It's garbage. It's a stitch up job," he added.He compared the charges against the men to the legal proceedings against US President Donald Trump - which he called a "witch hunt" - and referred to the attempted extradition as "political kidnapping".When asked how long the extradition proceedings would take, McBride estimated ahead of the hearing that it would be "around 60 days".Durham Law School Associate Professor Gemma Davies said there was a clause in the US-UK treaty that prevents extradition for political offences, adding lawyers for the Tates could argue the allegations are politically motivated."However, it's very rare extradition between the UK and US is refused on those grounds," Davies said, adding the test would be subject to a high threshold.She said it was "relatively rare" for an extradition request between the two countries to be refused given the strong working relationship between authorities."It's fairly common to extradite between the two, which means it's well traversed from a legal perspective, and we are two countries that have fairly similar legal systems in terms of criminal justice," Davies said.But Bill Bowring, Emeritus Professor of Law at Birkbeck College, University of London, said the ongoing proceedings in Romania were a "major complication".He said he had not "seen a satisfactory answer" as to why the CPS was now pursuing extradition from the US after a Romanian court ruled in 2024 the pair could only be extradited from that country after the conclusion of their separate case there.The BBC has requested comment from the CPS in relation to its position on the Romanian proceedings.Davies said that if the CPS was successful, the extradition could still take months, with any appeal delaying the process by about a year, "if not more".

schema.headline: Will the Tate brothers be extradited from the US to the UK?

title: Newspaper headlines: 'Burnham era begins' and 'King Kev'
description: Pictures of the UK's new prime minister outside No 10 are plastered across the front pages. The Guardian says Andy Burnham's arrival in Downing Street
body: Pictures of the UK's new prime minister outside No 10 are plastered across the front pages. The Guardian says Andy Burnham's arrival in Downing Street gives Labour "an opportunity for a second hearing with the public, external, but could also unleash yet more instability".The Daily Mail calls the purge of many of Sir Keir Starmer's allies from the cabinet "the night of the long eyelashes", external, referencing Burnham's much-discussed winkers.  The Financial Times notes that appointing John Healey as chancellor may come with a "hefty price tag", external, due to the former defence secretary's previous demands for more military spending.There are contradictory reports as to whether the new prime minister will bring in a 50p income tax rate for high earners, to fund a potential increase in the personal allowance for people on lower incomes. The i Paper says the idea hasn't been ruled out., external But according to the Times, his allies have insisted that he won't introduce the higher rate. , externalThe editorials are mixed about the prime minister's first day in the job. The Guardian compares his messaging to the post-1945 Labour government, external, which it says "delivered lasting progress through a revitalised state".  But the Daily Mail likens it to "a bad 1970s tribute act", external, complete with "renationalisation, reindustrialisation" and a "bigger and more meddlesome state". The Daily Express says the prime minister needs a "reality check", external. It calls for every decision to be "properly costed and fully funded." The Daily Mirror notes his promise of cost of living support offers "hope to millions", external.Away from politics, the Sun devotes its front page to the former England, Liverpool and Newcastle footballer Kevin Keegan, whose death at the age of 75 was announced on Monday. The paper describes him as a "fearless superstar and idol to millions"., external The Guardian says as a player he was a "bundle of energy with a predator's opportunism"., external The Times says he was an "effervescent figure" whose "impact crossed cultural boundaries"., external

schema.headline: Newspaper headlines: 'Burnham era begins' and 'King Kev'

title: The 20-somethings betting big on tech stocks
description: In her teens, Michelle Huynh, the eldest daughter of migrant parents in Australia, made a promise to her family that she would become a millionaire by
body: In her teens, Michelle Huynh, the eldest daughter of migrant parents in Australia, made a promise to her family that she would become a millionaire by the time she turned 30.The 26-year-old describes it as "a somewhat silly promise" inspired by the sacrifices her non-English-speaking parents made to raise the family.But she is trying to make that dream come true by investing her savings in the stock market."Times are so different and investing has become a necessity," says Huynh, who works in sales for a tech firm. "It feels like our purchasing power is shrinking. This is the only way to combat that."This year, the technology-driven surge in stock markets has edged her closer to that goal.With more than a third of her investments in tech stocks, by the middle of July that part of her savings had jumped this year by 50% - a rise of A$31,000 (£16,100; $21,666).But those gains have now eased to about A$22,000 as the sector is going through what she calls a "wild moment".Huynh says she's prepared for the volatility, viewing those investments as a long-term bet.The rise in tech stocks, led by firms riding the artificial intelligence (AI) boom, has attracted large numbers of ordinary investors, many of them in their 20s and early 30s, even as some analysts warn that the fervour around AI may be overblown.Retail investors have been caught up in the excitement, which has been fuelled by social media and marketing efforts to draw non-professionals, says Glenn Tan from advisory firm Providend.The tech-heavy Nasdaq in the US is up by about 10% this year, while Japan's Nikkei 225 has risen by more than 20%.But many tech stocks have seen big swings - both up and down.That volatility is most apparent in South Korea. Seoul's Kospi index, which includes tech heavyweights like SK Hynix and Samsung Electronics, has jumped by more than 50% since January.The rally has attracted an army of retail investors, known locally as "ants", which has helped fuel volatile trading."I could maybe count with my hands the number of people who aren't investing today," South Korean investor U Chan Lee says. "Even stay-at-home mothers, like my mum, who has never been interested in the stock market, are now interested."But the Kospi has seen some dramatic moves. Since hitting a record high of more than 9,000 points in June it has plunged to around 6,500.Trading on the benchmark index has been halted seven times this year under a mechanism called the circuit breaker, designed to calm panic selling after it fell by 8%.Those slides have raised concerns over people who have borrowed money to invest in stocks, leading to South Korean authorities taking action to curb the practice.Lee, 30, sold many of his shares last year when the Kospi surged, concerned the market was becoming "too overheated". Since then, he has shifted to buying stocks when they fall and selling them when they rise a few days later.Singaporean business student Shyan Lim is a firm believer in AI-related shares, putting about three-quarters of his savings into tech stocks.The 24-year-old says there have been plenty of "uneasy" days when his investments plunged by as much as 10%, but he is willing to take his chances.Overall, his bets have worked so far. In October, he invested 23,000 Singapore dollars (£13,185; $17,845) in chipmakers Intel and Micron. Those shares are now worth about 100,000 Singapore dollars."It feels like I'm one step closer to retirement," Lim says. "While I'm still young I think I can take the risk. I probably won't take such positions when I'm older."With more than half of his investments in tech, George Lee, who recently graduated, says he is well aware of the lack of resources and other disadvantages that ordinary traders can face."Investing doesn't just come down to luck, though it does play a part," Lee says."As long as the fundamentals of a company don't change, I'm ready for the swings since I have time to let my shares run."

schema.headline: The 20-somethings betting big on tech stocks

title: Key takeaways as Andy Burnham becomes PM
description: Image source, APByPaul SeddonPolitical reporterAndy Burnham has replaced Sir Keir Starmer as UK prime minister, after becoming Labour leader last week
body: Image source, APByPaul SeddonPolitical reporterAndy Burnham has replaced Sir Keir Starmer as UK prime minister, after becoming Labour leader last week.The former mayor of Greater Manchester becomes the country's fourth prime minister in five years and the seventh since 2016.Burnham says rough sleeping and the cost of living are prioritiesIn his first speech in Downing Street, Burnham announced he would "end rough sleeping in our country".This was swiftly followed by a No 10 press release promising an extra £340m for the task over the next five years from within the housing department budget.He also promised a new 10-year plan for Britain - to be unveiled later this year - and to build a new economy, putting "life's essentials back under public control".But arguing that people also needed "breathing space now", he said announcements on tackling the cost of living would start on Tuesday.Other changes he promised included changing the education system to help young people into work, building more council homes, and honouring commitments to fund defence while sticking to the UK's spending and debt rules.Cabinet picksBurnham made top appointments to his cabinet, making John Healey his chancellor in an move that took Westminster by surprise.Rachel Reeves had already confirmed she would be leaving the role and would not be serving in Burnham's cabinet, turning down the offer of another job.In other top moves, former energy secretary Ed Miliband has been promoted to foreign secretary, with Shabana Mahmood reappointed home secretary.Louise Haigh, a key ally who oversaw his preparations for No 10, has been given the job of running the Cabinet Office and appointed first secretary of state.Appointments, including to roles below the cabinet rank, are expected to continue on Tuesday. The new PM didn't use a lecternImage source, EPA/ShutterstockThere was much that was familiar about Burnham's speech in front of the famous black No 10 door, which was thronged with staffers and supporters. But perhaps conscious that "lectern moments" have become a symbol of the UK's political instability in recent years, Burnham broke with recent tradition and made the address without notes or a podium.He said that he was "acutely conscious" of the churn in the office behind him, stressing that the UK badly needed to regain stability.This would require politicians to "make politics work better" and "raise our game and rise to the new challenge", he added.Andy Burnham: Who is the new PM?From Manchester mayor to prime minister; what is his record and what does he stand for?Starmer and Burnham visited the KingKing Charles III said his farewells to Sir Keir in a final private audience at Buckingham Palace, before greeting Burnham.His role was central to the day because prime ministers are formally appointed by the sitting monarch, who is head of state.The short audience is known as "kissing hands" - although in practice, these days male premiers simply bow and shake hands with the monarch after accepting their offer to form a government.Image source, PA MediaNo hard feelings from StarmerEarlier, Sir Keir gave a short departure speech in Downing Street.With his supporters lining the street, he repeated key themes from the speech announcing his resignation in June, such as turning around the fortunes of the Labour Party and lowering child poverty.Despite leaving just two years after his landslide victory at the 2024 election, he insisted that he was proud of his record in office, saying Britain was "now stronger and fairer"."I go with good grace, I go with a smile," he said. He had time for a hug afterwards with Rachel Reeves, his chancellor and Downing Street neighbour during his time in office. Image source, EPA/ShutterstockGet in touchWhat questions do you have about the new PM and his policies?

schema.headline: Key takeaways as Andy Burnham becomes PM

title: Mammalian meat allergy: The allergy that can make daily activities deadly
description: When Myfanwy Webb looks back at her birthday party five years ago, she is overcome with regret - remembering what she now sees as a missed opportunity
body: When Myfanwy Webb looks back at her birthday party five years ago, she is overcome with regret - remembering what she now sees as a missed opportunity to save her son's life.Hours after a celebratory lunch at a restaurant Jeremy began having difficulty breathing. His parents called an ambulance, and the then-15-year-old began vomiting.At the hospital, doctors noted that Jeremy had a suspected meat allergy, but ultimately blamed the episode on his asthma, which he'd had since he was a toddler. He was sent home the next day."I made the wrong decision," Webb says, wishing she had asked for more testing. If she had, doctors might have established that Jeremy had actually been suffering from anaphylaxis, and sent him home with an EpiPen.​​Just over a year later, in June 2022, Jeremy died after eating a beef sausage on a camping trip with friends at a beach north of Sydney.The cause of death was initially recorded simply as asthma. But in February, after an inquest pushed for by his family, a coroner ruled that the asthma attack and resulting fatal anaphylaxis was triggered by tick-induced mammalian meat allergy (MMA).His death is the first in the world known to have been caused by MMA, also known as alpha-gal syndrome.Diagnosed cases of MMA have steadily escalated since 2007, when an Australian doctor first discovered the allergy was linked to tick bites. Now, scientists and campaigners are warning that the condition, though easily prevented, can be unexpectedly triggered - and could pose a deadly threat.Webb had long suspected that her son had a meat allergy, but it was never formally diagnosed and the family had no idea it could be fatal."When I looked up the research about it, there wasn't at that time anything to suggest it was more than a food intolerance."In retrospect, she says, it was "like a ticking time bomb".Symptoms of the allergy can range from mild to severe including skin rashes, swelling, stomach cramps, vomiting and anaphylaxis, which can be life-threatening.From around the age of 10 Jeremy had suffered nausea after eating meat, or even occasionally after inhaling the fumes from roast meat – which Webb believes may have set off the episode after her birthday party. On another occasion he had been hospitalised after eating marshmallows, which usually contain gelatin derived from animal parts.It was Prof Sheryl van Nunen who discovered the link between MMA and ticks almost 20 years ago, after noticing a correlation in patients at her Sydney allergy clinic.Working on Australia's east coast, she inadvertently found herself in one of the world's two MMA hotspots. The other is in the US - although cases of the allergy have since been noted on all six continents where ticks live.In Australia, MMA is caused by the bite of the eastern paralysis tick. After two tick bites 50% of people will develop antibodies to alpha-gal, a sugar molecule found in the tick's saliva, van Nunen explains. After that, they have a 15 to 20% chance of becoming allergic to meat."If only people knew what a tick could do," she says.Jeremy, who grew up surrounded by dense bushland at his family home north of Sydney, had been bitten by ticks since he was a small boy.It's not clear why the east coast, particularly areas north of Sydney, is an MMA hotspot - in some areas there are more than 700 sufferers per 100,000 people. But there are several hypotheses, says Dr Alex Gofton, a tick expert who led a recent study into the allergy.One is that more people are coming into contact with ticks there."The population in those tick endemic areas over the last 20, 30 years has increased dramatically," Gofton says.The years between 2020 and 2023 were also rainy La Nina years - conditions in which ticks thrive - and around the same time the Covid pandemic was pushing even more people to move out of cities and into the bush, he adds.The high rate of diagnosis - there has been a 22% increase in cases every year since 2020 - may also be fuelled by increased awareness and testing, Gofton says. Using lab data, he has concluded there are more than 5,000 suspected MMA cases in Australia - although he believes this is an under representation of the true figures.Despite increasing awareness, diagnosing a meat allergy can be difficult.Symptoms often develop at night, hours after people have eaten their evening meal, and for those suffering asthma, the two can often be confused.Van Nunen describes it as "an anytime but not an every time allergy".Symptoms may sometimes not appear at all, while at other times they may be exacerbated by factors including the type of meat, how it is cooked and if it is spicy, as well as whether a person drinks alcohol with it, exercises after eating or is sleep deprived.Those who are hypersensitive can also be triggered by mammalian products such as milk and anything that contains a derivative of mammal meat - beauty products and laundry softeners containing lanolin, for example, and even supermarket plastic bags containing stearic acid. Fumes from a barbecue, or petting a cat or dog, can also trigger an allergic reaction.Elizabeth Rowley, a mother of three who lives in Hannam Vale on the mid north coast of New South Wales, spent years going to doctors and allergy specialists, hoping they could diagnose the mystery illness that plagued her.But many dismissed her symptoms, which began in 2006 and ranged from memory loss and brain fog to chest pain, numbness in her arms, full body aches, and breathing difficulties that led to her regularly passing out."[They said] 'you're turning into a bit of a hypochondriac - it's not anaphylaxis - get over it'," she recalls.She has since set up a Facebook group to support fellow sufferers."It's just mentally horrendous more than anything, just trying to find out what it was," she says. "I thought I was going crazy."Remy Williams, a 23-year-old psychology student who lives in Sydney's Northern Beaches and was diagnosed with the allergy as a child, says the constant vigilance of watching what she ate tipped over into an eating disorder.But the even bigger impact, she says, was witnessing the effect on her mother, who also had the allergy and twice suffered severe anaphylactic attacks on aeroplanes.It was "incredibly scary," says Remy. She "literally almost died in front of me."Faced with so many potential triggers, everyday life can become tricky to navigate.Rowley struggled to find work as a bookkeeper because so many locations triggered her allergies. Jobs at shopping centres were ruled out because of the fumes from restaurants, and even in smaller environments her colleagues' eating habits could affect her. One boss's morning toast habit, she says, became a daily trial."As soon as the butter hits it, I just go…" she groans. "But you can't keep walking in and saying to the bosses, 'Sorry, you can't eat that'."Socialising can also be difficult, given that many events and gatherings centre around food."You can't do barbecues," says Rowley. "Can't do some restaurants either, or most restaurants."Van Nunen's biggest concern, though, is the 85% of people who have the allergy without knowing it – people who have developed the antibodies but who do not react after eating meat.Since MMA can rear its head unexpectedly, these people could still react to medical treatment, van Nunen explains, because many drugs include products derived from animals, such as vaccines that contain gelatin.At least one person is known to have died after receiving the drug cetuximab to treat colon cancer and going into anaphylaxis. Another died after being given a blood transfusion.Van Nunen says it is "well known" there have been more deaths from MMA. The best documented, however, are that of an American airline pilot who died hours after eating a hamburger in 2024, and Jeremy's.The key point to take away from a death like Jeremy's, Van Nunen says, is that 94.8% of people who die from food allergy anaphylaxis die because it triggers their asthma."They go straight into status asthmaticus and… unless you intubate them within a certain time, if they have a respiratory arrest, you have four minutes, otherwise they're brain dead," she explains.One of the things that has given Webb strength since her son's death is remembering the "positive spin" Jeremy put on life. "He fitted everything into it, wouldn't waste a minute," she says.That has made her determined to raise awareness of the allergy that took his life and improve treatment protocols, so that other families don't have to experience the same pain.In addition to speaking publicly about Jeremy's death, Webb has cooperated with the hospital where he was treated to help develop training on MMA. She is also working on a paper with van Nunen, which is due to be presented at a conference later this year."Now [Jeremy's] positive spin is actually helping people, even though he's passed," she says. "I think he'd be really pleased with us."How to avoid tick bites:Cover up when you're in bushland, tuck your socks into your trousers and wear light coloured clothing so you can spot ticks easilyDon't lie directly or picnic on the groundWear insecticides containing DEET or picaridinCheck yourself for ticks after walking in tick territory, and don't forget to check your petsIf you get bitten by a tick, remember "freeze don't squeeze", external - use a spray to kill the tick and let it drop off by itself rather than using tweezers, which can cause the ticks to expel toxins into the bloodstreamIf you don't have an ether containing spray, seek medical help to remove the tick

schema.headline: Mammalian meat allergy: The allergy that can make daily activities deadly

title: World Cup 2026: Fifa opens investigation into Argentina post-final trouble
description: Argentina will face an investigation into the ugly scenes at the end of the World Cup final, Fifa has confirmed.Fifa's disciplinary committee has appo
body: Argentina will face an investigation into the ugly scenes at the end of the World Cup final, Fifa has confirmed.Fifa's disciplinary committee has appointed a disciplinary and ethics prosecutor to assess what happened after the final whistle at MetLife stadium.Any player or coach involved in the fracas could face suspension, while the Argentine Football Association (AFA) may also be fined.Following their 1-0 defeat on Sunday, members of the Argentina team and backroom staff were involved in altercations with Spain players as they began to celebrate their World Cup success.Nahuel Molina, Leandro Paredes, Thiago Almada and assistant coach Roberto Ayala all appeared to be involved. It was initially reported by Fifa that Paredes had been sent off by referee Slavko Vincic after he pushed Spain's Eric Garcia by the throat. Paredes' red card was removed from the record shortly afterwards and Fifa has confirmed to BBC Sport that no disciplinary action was taken at the time.Argentina had already had one player dismissed, with Enzo Fernandez receiving a second yellow card in second-half stoppage time. Fifa had already begun the process of potential disciplinary action against Argentina after their players held a banner in support of their country's claims to the Falkland Islands following the 2-1 semi-final win against England.As the final whistle blew in New Jersey, a few Argentina players - including Lionel Messi - slumped to the turf.Others stood there motionless as Spain's players raced across the pitch in jubilation.Rodri, who had been substituted in extra-time, ran over from the dugout to join his team-mates. As the Manchester City midfielder went past Molina, the Argentina defender appeared to punch Rodri on the arm or stomach. This was the fuse which ignited the trouble.Rodri confronted Molina, but it was the Argentina player who thrust forward in an aggressive manner.   Garcia ran over to back up his team-mate at which point Paredes got involved too, jabbing Garcia in the throat.Unused substitute Gavi tried to intervene, only to be forced to the ground by Almada.Paredes then pushed Gavi to the floor by putting his hand in the Spaniard's face. Ayala then looked to be holding Garcia around the throat. He then appeared to strike Spain midfielder Dani Olmo."Paredes threw two or three haymakers into someone's face. He went for them," Alan Shearer said on BBC One's coverage. "There is no place or space for that."We know how much it means to them and we know how much it hurts to lose, but there is a way to lose. Too many times we have seen that from Argentina. The reaction after the final whistle is terrible."Joe Hart added: "The game's done, they can have absolutely no complaints about what happened today. Disgusting behaviour."But Argentina boss Lionel Scaloni, who had attempted to calm the situation, appeared to play down the incidents in his post-match interview."We are gracious in victory, and we must be gracious in defeat," he said. "Today, we are showing that we know how to lose."We lost the match, and we accept it, but that doesn't mean we stop living or forget everything we did to get here."Fifa's disciplinary committee has a number of options open to it.By appointing a disciplinary and ethics prosecutor, Fifa will look in more detail at offences rather than just potential violent conduct.There is a greater chance of stronger punishment if players or the national association is charged and found guilty.Article 13 of the disciplinary code covers offensive behaviour and the principles of fair play.The actions of the players and staff could be judged as "violating the basic rules of decent conduct" and "behaving in a way that brings the sport of football and/or Fifa into disrepute".The disciplinary and ethics prosecutor will take into account "both aggravating and mitigating circumstances", and it has the power to enforce a ban globally.Fifa used this clause to charge then-Spanish football federation president Luis Rubiales after kissing Jenni Hermoso on the lips after Spain's Women's World Cup final win against England.Rubiales was banned from all football-related activities for three years in 2023 and lost an appeal to get it overturned.Uruguay striker Luis Suarez was suspended from all football-related activity for four months for biting Italy defender Giorgio Chiellini at the 2014 World Cup.It meant the Liverpool player missed the first nine games of the Premier League season.It is unlikely that Almada, Ayala, Molina and Paredes will face bans as long as this.The AFA, however, has faced issues with the behaviour of its players in recent years.Argentina faced charges related to "offensive behaviour and violations of the principles of fair play" and "misconduct of players and officials" following the 2022 World Cup final.The South Americans beat France on penalties but were criticised for the way they celebrated.Aston Villa goalkeeper Emiliano Martinez made a lewd gesture with his Goalkeeper of the Tournament trophy, before being filmed in the dressing room mocking France star Kylian Mbappe.Argentina were fined £18,300 and no action was taken against any player involved. But Martinez was banned for two games in 2024 for 'offensive behaviour' in World Cup qualifiers against Chile and Colombia.Martinez repeated his infamous celebration using a replica Copa America trophy following a 3-0 win over Chile. Four days later, he hit a camera with his gloves as a cameraman approached him on the pitch after a 2-1 defeat by Colombia.Following Argentina's 2024 Copa America win, Chelsea midfielder Enzo Fernandez was forced to apologise for a video he posted. The French Football Federation said the song was a "racist and discriminatory" chant.At this World Cup, the AFA is already facing a probable fine for the banner held up after the England game.Their players celebrated while holding a cloth which read "Las Malvinas son Argentinas", which translates as "The Falklands are Argentine".In 2014, the AFA was fined £20,000 after its players held up a banner with the same message before a friendly against Slovenia.It is possible that Fifa could issue bans to the players who were holding it: Tottenham's Cristian Romero, former Spurs player Giovani Lo Celso and Manchester United's Lisandro Martínez.After Spain's 2-1 Euro 2024 final win over England, their captain Alvaro Morata and team-mate Rodri were banned for one game by Uefa after they chanted "Gibraltar is Spanish" during their victory celebrations.There is no timescale for Fifa's prosecutor to complete his investigation into Argentina's conduct.On 18 February, Uefa appointed an ethics and disciplinary inspector to look into alleged racist abuse by Gialunca Prestianni to Real Madrid's Vinicius Jr during Real Madrid's 1-0 win against Benfica.It was over two months, on 24 April, before Prestianni was given a six-game ban for homophobic conduct, with three matches suspended for two years.

schema.headline: World Cup 2026: Fifa opens investigation into Argentina post-final trouble

title: Fire crews to remain at Arthur's Seat for days after blaze
description: Image source, Craig Duncan PhotographyImage caption, Police closed the road surrounding Arthur's Seat as firefighters tackled the fireUpdated 20 July
body: Image source, Craig Duncan PhotographyImage caption, Police closed the road surrounding Arthur's Seat as firefighters tackled the fireUpdated 20 July 2026Firefighters are expected to remain at Arthur's Seat in Edinburgh "for a couple of days" following a large blaze on Sunday evening.Small plumes of smoke could still be seen on Monday morning along Salisbury Crags and large swathes of vegetation below the cliff face had been burned black.Six fire engines were sent to tackle the the grassfire on the slope between the Scottish Parliament and St Leonard's, with crews working overnight to douse the flames and dampen hotspots. The Scottish Fire and Rescue Service (SFRS) said there were "no reported casualties" but Police Scotland said Queen's Drive, the road that encircles the Holyrood Park hills, remained closed to all vehicles.It is the second fire in the last three months at the landmark, an ancient extinct volcano that offers panoramic views of the Scottish capital.  A Very High risk of wildfire warning for the central Highlands, southern and eastern Scotland has been extended to Friday as warm weather is forecast to continue.The warning was extended by the SFRS, in conjunction with the Scottish Wildfire Forum (SWF). Figure caption, Fire crews tackle blaze on Arthur’s Seat in EdinburghEdinburgh University student Mahek Jayantilial, who lives near Arthur's Seat, said she could see the fire from her window."I was a bit worried because I thought the fire would come this way, but thankfully there's enough concrete here to stop it," the 24-year-old said.Jayantilial said it was "upsetting" to see Arthur's Seat on fire but she believed it was almost unavoidable as there had not been much rain recently and the grass had dried out."It was bound to happen," she added."A small bit of glass or a poorly snuffed-out cigarette butt would have ignited it, and then something so small creates such a big fire."The sound of chainsaws could be heard on Monday morning, believed to be from work to clear vegetation. On the ground, firefighters were concentrating efforts on the area of Queen's Drive closest to the Pollock Halls of Residence, the largest student accommodation site for Edinburgh University. Image source, PA MediaImage caption, Student Mahek Jayantilial said the grass had dried out from the lack of rainfallImage source, Malcolm McFadyenImage caption, Vegetation on the hillside burned as the fire spreadJon Henderson, SFRS assistant chief officer, said the service had dealt with a number of incidents over the course of the weekend. "We had crews at Arthur's Seat on Sunday evening and then overnight," he told BBC Radio Scotland Breakfast."At first light we stepped up that response again when the conditions were favourable for that."The feeling from the ground there is that we will probably be there for a couple of days continuing to tackle it and dampen out hotspots."Henderson said it was too early to speculate on the cause of the fire.  "Our focus at the moment is dealing with the fire and the response but there will be a full fire investigation," he said. "The difficulty we have with open fires is that they can be difficult to assess where they started because of the scale and sometimes these do happen naturally."A spokesperson from Historic Environment Scotland, which manages the park, said: "Some flare-ups have been reported today, as hotspots remain beneath the surface and sometimes reignite as temperatures increase or wind conditions change."With hot and dry weather increasing the risk of wildfires, we urge everyone to take extra care - please do not light fires, disposable barbecues or leave litter, including glass bottles and cigarette ends, which can increase the risk of fire."Image gallerySkip image galleryImage source, Craig Duncan PhotographyImage caption, A large plume of smoke could be seen from as far as Fife on Sunday eveningSlide 1 of 6, An aerial shot of white plumes of smoke coming from Arthur's Seat. , A large plume of smoke could be seen from as far as Fife on Sunday eveningCallum Smith, who also lives near Holyrood Park, said he was concerned about the impact the fire will have on a nearby peregrine falcon nesting site."Peregrine falcons have been nesting here for four, five, six years, possibly longer," he said.Smith said there were two adults and two chicks in a nest where the fire was along the cliffs and the youngest chick had not fledged yet."Possibly the two adults and the eldest chick would have been able to fly away, but the outcome for the youngest one is unclear," he added."Unfortunately, the smoke traversed over the top of the cliffs and onto the cliffs, so the smoke would have gone directly onto the nest site."Image source, PA MediaImage caption, Callum Smith said he was worried about the impact of the fire on wildlife in the areaCairngorms wildfireIt is the latest callout for the Scottish fire service during the recent stretch of warm, dry weather which has seen a heightened risk of wildfires. Hundreds of firefighters, two helicopters and specialist resources were sent to the Cairngorms National Park where a blaze has been ongoing for five days.Six square kilometres of land have been affected resulting in homes and businesses, including campsites and a ski resort in the Glenmore Forest Park area, being evacuated as a safety precaution.A high volume pump has been used to draw water from Loch Morlich to support firefighting efforts.On Sunday, fire crews said the fire was now "surrounded" and police began escorting residents and campers back to properties and campsites to collect personal belongings.Jon Henderson said resources remained on site but added that "significant progress" had been made."The activity over the rest of the day is to maintain a four kilometre long fire break and to search out and extinguish hotspots," he added.Get involvedYour news, your pictures

schema.headline: Fire crews to remain at Arthur's Seat for days after blaze

title: US launches fresh strikes on Iran, as Trump warns of retaliation for deaths of soldiers
description: The US military says it launched another round of strikes against Iran to "further degrade" its capabilities to attack ships in the Strait of Hormuz.T
body: The US military says it launched another round of strikes against Iran to "further degrade" its capabilities to attack ships in the Strait of Hormuz.The strikes - the 10th consecutive night of attacks - come after US President Donald Trump vowed to punish Iran for the deaths of US soldiers.Trump earlier said Iran had been hit "very hard" the previous night "in honour" of three US soldiers killed recently - two in Jordan and one in Iraq. In response to the latest wave, Iran said it struck two oil tankers in the waterway, as well as US military assets in Bahrain and Kuwait, state news agency IRNA reported. President Masoud Pezeshkian said Iran was now fighting a "full-scale war" with the US.At the same time, Tehran said diplomatic messages were still being exchanged by both sides through intermediaries, despite a collapse of a ceasefire.US Central Command (Centcom) said on X that the latest strikes had hit Iranian "military command centres, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites, and air defense systems". Commercial vessels have continued to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, Centcom said.Iran said rescue teams were removing the crews from the two oil tankers it had struck while attempting to transit through the vital waterway, according to the state news agency IRNA. The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations agency (UKMTO) said a tanker in the Strait of Hormuz had reported being struck by an unknown projectile, forcing its crew to abandon the vessel and board a lifeboat. It was not clear whether the tanker reported by UKMTO was among those cited by Iran.On Tuesday night the Department of Defense confirmed the identities of two of the US soldiers – Pte Isabella Gonzales, 19, from Texas, and Lt Tyler James Feehan, 25, from Hawaii - who were killed in an Iranian strike on the Muwaffaq Salti base in Jordan over the weekend.Centcom said earlier a third service member also went missing in action after the attack, and unidentified remains were later found in the area.Elsewhere in the region, Jordan's military said late on Monday it had shot down three Iranian missiles as sirens sounded across the country warning of incoming fire.Earlier in the day, Kuwait's armed forces said they had intercepted Iranian drones.Bahrain said drones launched by Iran had targeted its air navigation systems - but traffic remained unaffected.On the US strikes the previous night, Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency reported that several cities had been hit including Tabriz, Chabahar, Konarak, Bandar Mahshahr and Bandar Imam Khomeini.Iranian state media reported that two oil tankers had exploded as the vessels tried to cross the strait.Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said the tankers were going through what it called an "unsafe and hazardous southern route" of the strait and "exploded and were brought to a halt". Centcom disputed the claims.

schema.headline: US launches fresh strikes on Iran, as Trump warns of retaliation for deaths of soldiers
